After last night, when eight members of the Democratic caucus joined Senate Republicans to advance a bill to reopen the government. Moments ago, President Trump saying he would abide by the deal, calling it very good. Senate Majority Leader Thune today urging lawmakers not to drag the process out.

It reverses the administration's shutdown layoffs and guarantees back pay for all federal furloughed employees. The deal prompting frustration among the majority of Democrats who voted against it, even as they stopped short of directly criticizing their colleagues.

Just a year ago, al-Shirah had a $10 million U.S. bounty on his head, but he's since risen to power in Syria after toppling the Assad regime. President Trump touted his meeting with the Syrian leader, calling him a strong leader, adding that the U.S. will help make Syria successful again.
SPEAKER_00: Joining me now is James Jeffrey. He's the former U.S. special representative for Syria engagement in President Trump's first term. He's also a distinguished fellow at the Washington Institute. James, thank you for joining me. What are your takeaways from this meeting today? And what message does it send to the Mideast region and also to President Putin, who backed Bashar al-Assad, to have the president of Syria at the White House today?
SPEAKER_02: One, most importantly, this is a great day for the Syrian people who have lived through almost 14 years of terrible civil war, outside intervention, 600,000 killed, half the population displaced, $200 to $300 billion worth of damage. And now the international community led by Trump is embracing them. Two, it is a victory for the American administration, Israel, Arab states, and Turkey, who over the last two years have defeated and pushed back Iran across the border in the Middle East, not just nuclear program and missiles, not just Gaza, but not just Lebanon, but also finally Syria. That's very important because the message Trump is sending and people are receiving it is we want to build a different, new, more peaceful Middle East. Three, it's a triumph for Trump. Six years ago, 3rd of September, 2018, Trump issued a public, very tough warning to Putin. Do not go into Idlib. That's where al-Shara, then al-Julani and his troops and three million refugees will hold up. Trump forced Putin to back down. Putin remembered what happened to the Wagner Brigade a few months earlier, and that kept...
SPEAKER_02: al-Shara and his people in the game opposing Assad, opposing the Iranians, opposing Russia until finally they could overthrow the entire government in December. So it's a good day for Trump as well.
SPEAKER_00: Let's talk more about al-Shara. He was once linked to al-Qaeda. Can the U.S. trust him? What's your assessment on how he's led Syria since the fall of Assad last year?
SPEAKER_02: What we always say in diplomacy is trust but verify. His troops twice have engaged in massacres of minority groups in the spring, the Alawites in the West in the summer, Druze in the South. The important thing is they then pulled back Afshara, disciplined some troops, opened investigations, and it hasn't been repeated. So, so far, so good. But ever since 9-11, we, beginning with the Bush administration, General Petraeus carried it out in Iraq, took the position. We are not going to say, once a terrorist, always a terrorist. If you want to convert, if you want to focus on fighting the bad guys rather than fighting us, we will partner with you. That's what Jelani did between 2013 and 2016. We then, by 2018, were starting to—
SPEAKER_02: carefully, indirectly partnered with him to keep him in the fight, provide assistance to the refugees under his care, and it worked out.
SPEAKER_00: We have only about 30 seconds for this answer, but what needs to happen there for there to be stability in Syria?
SPEAKER_02: First of all, the international community led by the United States has to have one single position towards al-Shara and the future of Syria. Thirdly, we have to resolve outstanding issues with the Kurds in the north and with Israel to the south. If we can do all of that, and it's a big agenda, we'll be OK.

SPEAKER_02: Thank you very much.

        *   Summary: Senator John Fetterman explains his decision to vote for the shutdown deal, emphasizing the need to reopen the government and pay federal workers over securing immediate ACA subsidies.
